Title: Genetic modulation of RAP alters fruit coloration in both wild and cultivated strawberry

Common_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: F. vesca: gene31672; F. × ananassa homeologs: FxaC_1-2g38550; FxaC_1-4g34540; FxaC_1-1g01220
Common_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Yellow Wonder 5AF7 / YW5AF7 (functional studies in rap myb10 background); Ruegen (WT comparison)
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytosol-to-vacuole transport pathway; facilitates anthocyanin sequestration into vacuole
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Fruit and vegetative tissue pigmentation/colour formation
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Fruit receptacle skin and flesh; leaf petioles; carpel stigma; vacuole
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: In WT F. vesca fruit, RAP is greatly induced at turning stage compared with anthesis
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Constitutively high in 35S:RAP transgenic F. vesca; native RAP knockout in cultivated strawberry causes loss of pigmentation in petioles and fruit
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV 35S constitutive promoter for RAP overexpression; Arabidopsis U6-26 and U6-29 promoters for sgRNAs in CRISPR/Cas9 construct; 35S::GFP marker cassette present in editing vector
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable overexpression in rap myb10; CRISPR/Cas9 knockout in octoploid cultivated strawberry; transient fruit expression; qRT-PCR; RNA-seq; HPLC anthocyanin profiling; total anthocyanin measurement; mutation genotyping by cloning/Sanger sequencing
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes anthocyanin accumulation and red coloration when overexpressed; knockout abolishes anthocyanin accumulation causing green petioles and white fruit
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Stable overexpression of RAP in F. vesca rap myb10 restored red petioles and unexpectedly produced red fruit despite the myb10 background. 2. RAP overexpression caused precocious anthocyanin accumulation from floral stage 9/anthesis through early fruit development and altered fruit anthocyanin composition, increasing peonidin-3-glucoside relative to WT. 3. RNA-seq showed anthocyanin biosynthetic genes were not transcriptionally up-regulated in RAP-ox; rap myb10, indicating a MYB10-independent mechanism likely via enhanced anthocyanin transport/flux redirection. 4. CRISPR/Cas9 simultaneously mutated multiple RAP copies in cultivated strawberry 'Ningyu', producing green stems/petioles and white fruits with dramatically reduced anthocyanin. 5. Some T1 progeny lost the CRISPR/Cas9 transgene but retained the edited green-stem trait, supporting breeding value.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Normal growth-room conditions; developmental stage strongly affects phenotype interpretation, especially anthesis/early fruit versus turning stage
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No major growth penalty reported; overexpression caused novel brown leaf spots and red carpel stigmas/ectopic pigmentation
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Anthocyanins (binding/transport substrate); functional context with FveMYB10-dependent anthocyanin pathway
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq of fruit receptacles/carpels at anthesis and turning stage, 12 libraries total (3 biological replicates per sample); accession number NA
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising target for strawberry fruit-colour breeding: overexpression can enhance/alter red pigmentation, while CRISPR knockout can generate white-fruited germplasm in cultivated strawberry, including transgene-free edited progeny
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Classified as Common Gene because RAP encodes a GST transporter rather than a biosynthetic enzyme or transcriptional regulator. The paper proposes RAP enhances vacuolar anthocyanin transport and may redirect metabolic flux from proanthocyanidin toward anthocyanin production at early developmental stages. Overexpression effect is independent of FveMYB10-mediated transcriptional activation at ripening.

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: YW5AF7; rap myb10; RAP-ox; rap myb10; Ruegen
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Fruit receptacle and vegetative pigmented tissues
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Not expressed at early fruit developmental stages but greatly induced during ripening/turning stage in red-fruited strawberry
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Mutated in Yellow Wonder background; expression not significantly changed in RNA-seq pairwise comparisons noted here
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Background mutant comparison; prior knowledge integrated in this study; RNA-seq expression analysis; transient co-overexpression context mentioned
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Positive regulator required for normal ripening-associated anthocyanin biosynthesis and red fruit coloration; natural mutation causes white fruit
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FveMYB10 is the known key positive transcription factor for strawberry fruit coloration. 2. The myb10 mutant background is white-fruited due to loss of ripening-associated anthocyanin pathway activation. 3. RAP overexpression restored fruit coloration in an FveMYB10-independent manner, highlighting that FveMYB10 is not the core manipulated gene in this paper but an essential background regulator.
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Anthocyanin biosynthetic gene network; likely MYB-bHLH-WD40 complex context
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq expression analysis; accession number NA
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Known fruit-colour regulator, but in this paper mainly serves as background context to demonstrate RAP-dependent coloration independent of MYB10
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as critical background regulator, not the primary experimental core gene of this study.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Branch allocation between proanthocyanidin and anthocyanin pigmentation
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Down-regulation redirects flux from proanthocyanidin to anthocyanin in immature strawberry fruit
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ANR is cited as prior evidence that reducing the proanthocyanidin branch can promote anthocyanin accumulation in immature receptacles. 2. It is used in this paper as mechanistic support for RAP-mediated flux redirection, not as a core experimentally tested gene here.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Background gene, not a core gene experimentally manipulated in this study.
